content.text: In production agentic AI systems, every LLM call consumes tokens billed at a price per million. When hundreds of agents run concurrently across multiple teams, costs accumulate quickly and the total invoice number from the provider gives no insight into which agent, task, user, or product feature drove the expense. Cost attribution is the discipline of tagging and tracking every inference cost back to the organizational unit that caused it.

Cost attribution is the practice of recording each billable AI event — an LLM API call, a tool invocation, an embedding request — alongside metadata that identifies its source: which agent made the call, which task it was executing, which user initiated the run, and which team or product owns that capability. The raw cost event (model name, input tokens, output tokens, timestamp) produced by the provider is enriched with these organizational dimensions and stored in a queryable form.
assigns cost to individual runs, tasks, and agents using unique identifiers propagated through the system as context.
rolls those events up by team, product, environment, or time window to produce cost-per-feature, cost-per-user, or cost-per-department figures useful for billing and capacity planning.

The accounting unit in LLM APIs is the token. Providers publish per-token prices that differ by model tier and by whether the tokens are in the input (prompt) or output (completion). A complete cost record for a single call therefore contains: model identifier, input token count, output token count, any cached token counts that attract a discounted rate, the price schedule applied, and the computed cost in the billing currency. Attribution adds: agent ID, task ID, user ID, team, environment, and any application-specific labels.

Without attribution, an AI platform's costs are a black box. The monthly invoice shows a total spend and a token count, but offers no answer to the questions that matter operationally: Is it the research agent or the code-review agent burning most of the budget? Which customer account is responsible for 30% of inference costs? Did the new feature launched last week cause the cost spike that appeared on Tuesday?

These questions have financial and product consequences. On the financial side, teams building multi-tenant SaaS products need to know the cost to serve each customer in order to price correctly, identify loss-making accounts, and implement fair-use limits. On the operational side, engineers need per-agent cost breakdowns to identify which prompts are verbose, which agents loop excessively, and where prompt caching could cut the bill in half.

Attribution also enables budget enforcement. A budget controller that knows the current rolling spend per agent or per team can rate-limit or gracefully degrade a specific agent without touching others. Without attribution, the only enforcement options are provider-level spending caps that cut off the entire platform regardless of which component caused the overrun.
Know exactly how much each agent, task type, team, and customer costs to serve. Replace a single opaque invoice line with a per-dimension cost breakdown queryable at any granularity.
Internal teams can be charged against their own AI budget. Product managers can track cost-per-feature. In SaaS products, per-customer cost data enables accurate per-seat or usage-based pricing.

Per-agent or per-team spending limits can be applied programmatically. When an agent approaches its budget, the system can switch to a cheaper model, reduce context size, or refuse to start new tasks — all without affecting other agents.

Attribution surfaces the highest-cost agent or task type. Once identified, that specific workflow is the high-value target for prompt compression, response caching, or model downgrade — delivering cost reductions without touching anything else.

A sudden cost spike for a specific agent can trigger an alert within minutes. Without attribution the same spike would be invisible until the monthly invoice, or until a provider-level alert fires for the entire account.
Cost-per-successful-task, cost-per-user-query, or cost-per-document-processed are metrics that connect AI spending to product value. These are only computable with attribution in place.

Instrumentation overhead is the primary cost of attribution. Every LLM call must carry a context object with attribution labels, and every response must be intercepted to extract token counts and record a cost event. In Python this typically means a wrapper or decorator around the SDK client, which adds a small synchronous overhead per call. For latency-sensitive pipelines this overhead is measurable, though it is rarely above 1–2 milliseconds.

Context propagation is structurally the hardest part of attribution. A long-running orchestration that spawns sub-agents must ensure that the originating task ID and user ID propagate into every child call. If a sub-agent creates its own LLM client without inheriting the parent's context, those calls are recorded as unattributed. In systems built without attribution from the start, retrofitting context propagation often requires touching every agent's call site.

A high-throughput agent platform making tens of thousands of LLM calls per day produces a proportionate volume of cost events. A cost record is small (under 1 KB) but the event stream must be stored durably for auditability. Plan for this in storage budget and retention policy.

Token prices differ by model and change when providers update their pricing. Cost computation logic must be updated whenever a new model or pricing tier is introduced. An outdated price table produces inaccurate cost figures without any error signal.

External sub-agents, SDKs, or LangChain components that make their own API calls may not propagate your attribution context. Costs incurred by components outside your control appear as unattributed spend.
Attribution does not capture opportunity cost.

A task that returns a poor result after spending $0.50 in tokens shows up correctly in cost attribution but the quality failure is invisible. Cost attribution must be paired with quality metrics to give a complete picture of value.

: a structured record emitted once per LLM API call. At minimum it carries the provider, model, input token count, output token count, and the computed cost. Attribution enriches it with organizational dimensions.
Token costs follow a simple linear formula. For a provider that charges
dollars per output token, the cost of a single call is:
are the input and output token counts from the provider's usage response. When prompt caching is in use, cached tokens attract a different (lower) rate

attrs.latex: \text{cost} = n_{\text{in, new}} \cdot p_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{in, cached}} \cdot p_{\text{cache}} + n_{\text{out}} \cdot p_{\text{out}}

content.text: This calculation runs in the cost telemetry layer immediately after each API response is received. The result, along with all attribution labels from the current context, is written as a cost event to the event stream.
Attribution labels travel through the system using

. The most robust approach is to model attribution as a context object passed explicitly through every agent, analogous to how distributed tracing propagates a trace ID. When an orchestrator spawns a sub-agent, it passes the same context object, so all sub-agent calls carry the parent task's labels. Libraries such as Python's
module provide a thread-local and async-safe mechanism for implicit context propagation without requiring every function to accept an explicit parameter.
Once events are in the event stream, they are aggregated by dimension to produce the metrics used for reporting and enforcement. Rolling aggregations by
produce the daily chargeback report. The budget controller queries the rolling sum for a given dimension and compares it against the configured limit.

attrs.alt: Cost attribution architecture: LLM providers and tool APIs feed cost events into the Cost Telemetry Layer, which passes them to the Attribution Engine for tagging. The Attribution Engine routes to the Cost Store, Budget Controller, and Reporting layer.

content.text: at the top emit raw token-usage data: LLM providers return input and output token counts in every API response; tool calls and infrastructure components have their own per-call pricing. The
is a middleware wrapper that intercepts every outbound API call, extracts usage data from the response, computes the dollar cost, and emits a cost event. The
enriches each event with organizational labels from the propagated context — agent ID, task ID, user, team — and evaluates budget rules. Downstream, cost events flow to three consumers: a
for raw persistence and rollups, a

Attribution is valuable in any system where multiple distinct workloads share a common LLM API account and costs matter. The decision criteria are simple: if the question "which part of my system is spending the most on LLM calls?" cannot be answered today, attribution is needed.
multiple agent types (research, code review, summarization, customer support) run on the same account and you need to understand per-agent costs to prioritize optimization.
serving multiple customers or internal teams and you need per-tenant cost data for pricing, billing, or internal chargebacks.
enforcing per-agent or per-team budget limits programmatically, rather than relying solely on a provider-level account cap.
instrumenting for anomaly detection — a runaway agent that loops or generates far more tokens than expected should trigger an alert within minutes, not at invoice time.
for single-purpose prototypes, single-developer projects, or research notebooks where all spend is self-attributed and costs are low enough to inspect from the provider dashboard directly.
Define the attribution context schema. Decide which dimensions are required: at minimum
, and application-specific labels as needed. Define this as a dataclass or TypedDict so every usage site is type-checked.

is the idiomatic choice for async code: set the attribution context once at the start of a request, and all coroutines that run within that context inherit it automatically without receiving it as a parameter.
Wrap the LLM client with a cost-tracking layer. Create a thin wrapper class (or use a monkey-patching hook) that intercepts
, compute the cost using the model's price schedule, read the current attribution context, and emit a cost event.

Maintain a price schedule. Store model pricing in a configuration file or small database table. Include input price, output price, and any cached-token price for each model. Add a version field so you can track when prices were last updated and query historical costs at the rates that applied at the time.

Emit cost events to a durable sink. For small deployments this can be a local SQLite or PostgreSQL table. For production at scale, emit events to a message queue (Kafka, SQS, or Pub/Sub) and consume them into a time-series store or data warehouse.

Implement budget enforcement. Create a budget table keyed by attribution dimension (agent ID, team). After each cost event is recorded, the budget controller reads the rolling sum for the relevant dimension and compares it against the configured limit. If the limit is exceeded, set a flag that the agent checks before each new LLM call.

Build reporting queries. Write SQL or data warehouse queries that aggregate cost events by dimension and time window. Expose these as a dashboard or schedule them as daily report emails. Ensure anomaly alerts are configured — a spike of more than 2x the seven-day moving average for any single agent should notify the on-call engineer.
Basic: cost tracking wrapper with ContextVar propagation

# cost_tracker.py — minimal cost-attribution wrapper for the Anthropic SDK
# pip install anthropic

from __future__ import annotations
import time
from contextvars import ContextVar
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
import anthropic

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Attribution context — set once per request, inherited by all coroutines
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class AttributionContext:
    agent_id: str
    task_id: str
    user_id: str = ""
    team: str = ""
    environment: str = "production"

_attribution_ctx: ContextVar[AttributionContext | None] = ContextVar(
    "attribution_ctx", default=None
)

def set_attribution(ctx: AttributionContext) -> None:
    _attribution_ctx.set(ctx)

def get_attribution() -> AttributionContext | None:
    return _attribution_ctx.get()

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Price schedule (USD per 1 000 000 tokens)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

PRICE_SCHEDULE: dict[str, dict[str, float]] = {
    "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022": {"input": 3.00, "output": 15.00, "cache_read": 0.30},
    "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022":  {"input": 0.80, "output": 4.00,  "cache_read": 0.08},
    "claude-3-opus-20240229":     {"input": 15.00, "output": 75.00, "cache_read": 1.50},
}

def compute_cost(model: str, usage: Any) -> float:
    """Return cost in USD for a single API call."""
    prices = PRICE_SCHEDULE.get(model, {"input": 0.0, "output": 0.0, "cache_read": 0.0})
    per_m = 1_000_000
    input_tokens   = getattr(usage, "input_tokens",  0) or 0
    output_tokens  = getattr(usage, "output_tokens", 0) or 0
    # cache_read_input_tokens is present on Anthropic responses when caching is active
    cache_read     = getattr(usage, "cache_read_input_tokens", 0) or 0
    # Cached tokens are already counted in input_tokens; subtract to avoid double-billing
    new_input = max(0, input_tokens - cache_read)
    return (
        new_input    * prices["input"]      / per_m +
        cache_read   * prices["cache_read"] / per_m +
        output_tokens * prices["output"]    / per_m
    )

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Cost event — one record per LLM call
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class CostEvent:
    timestamp: float
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    cost_usd: float
    agent_id: str
    task_id: str
    user_id: str
    team: str
    environment: str

# In production, replace this with a write to your database or event queue.
_cost_log: list[CostEvent] = []

def _emit(event: CostEvent) -> None:
    _cost_log.append(event)
    print(
        f"[COST] agent={event.agent_id} task={event.task_id} "
        f"model={event.model} in={event.input_tokens} out={event.output_tokens} "
        f"cost=${event.cost_usd:.6f}"
    )

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Instrumented client wrapper
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

class TrackedAnthropicClient:
    """Drop-in wrapper around anthropic.Anthropic that emits a CostEvent per call."""

    def __init__(self, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        self._client = anthropic.Anthropic(**kwargs)
        self.messages = _TrackedMessages(self._client, self)


class _TrackedMessages:
    def __init__(self, client: anthropic.Anthropic, tracker: TrackedAnthropicClient) -> None:
        self._client = client
        self._tracker = tracker

    def create(self, **kwargs: Any) -> Any:
        response = self._client.messages.create(**kwargs)
        ctx = get_attribution()
        cost = compute_cost(kwargs.get("model", ""), response.usage)
        event = CostEvent(
            timestamp=time.time(),
            model=kwargs.get("model", "unknown"),
            input_tokens=getattr(response.usage, "input_tokens", 0) or 0,
            output_tokens=getattr(response.usage, "output_tokens", 0) or 0,
            cost_usd=cost,
            agent_id=ctx.agent_id   if ctx else "unattributed",
            task_id=ctx.task_id     if ctx else "unattributed",
            user_id=ctx.user_id     if ctx else "",
            team=ctx.team           if ctx else "",
            environment=ctx.environment if ctx else "unknown",
        )
        _emit(event)
        return response


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Usage
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

if __name__ == "__main__":
    set_attribution(AttributionContext(
        agent_id="research-agent-v2",
        task_id="task-42",
        user_id="user-123",
        team="product",
    ))

    client = TrackedAnthropicClient()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize the water cycle in two sentences."}],
    )
    print(response.content[0].text)
Intermediate: budget enforcement with per-agent rolling limit

# budget_controller.py — enforce per-agent spending limits using cost events
# Assumes cost events are written to a SQLite DB by the TrackedAnthropicClient above.

from __future__ import annotations
import sqlite3
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

DB_PATH = "costs.db"

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Schema setup (run once at startup)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def init_db(path: str = DB_PATH) -> sqlite3.Connection:
    conn = sqlite3.connect(path, check_same_thread=False)
    conn.execute("""
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cost_events (
            id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
            ts          REAL NOT NULL,
            agent_id    TEXT NOT NULL,
            task_id     TEXT NOT NULL,
            user_id     TEXT,
            team        TEXT,
            model       TEXT NOT NULL,
            input_tok   INTEGER NOT NULL,
            output_tok  INTEGER NOT NULL,
            cost_usd    REAL NOT NULL
        )
    """)
    conn.execute("CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_agent_ts ON cost_events (agent_id, ts)")
    conn.commit()
    return conn


def insert_cost_event(conn: sqlite3.Connection, event: dict) -> None:
    conn.execute("""
        INSERT INTO cost_events (ts, agent_id, task_id, user_id, team, model, input_tok, output_tok, cost_usd)
        VALUES (:ts, :agent_id, :task_id, :user_id, :team, :model, :input_tok, :output_tok, :cost_usd)
    """, event)
    conn.commit()


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Budget controller
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class BudgetRule:
    agent_id: str
    window_seconds: int    # rolling window, e.g. 3600 for 1 hour
    limit_usd: float


class BudgetController:
    def __init__(self, conn: sqlite3.Connection, rules: list[BudgetRule]) -> None:
        self._conn = conn
        self._rules: dict[str, BudgetRule] = {r.agent_id: r for r in rules}

    def rolling_cost(self, agent_id: str, window_seconds: int) -> float:
        """Sum of costs for agent_id within the last window_seconds."""
        since = time.time() - window_seconds
        row = self._conn.execute(
            "SELECT COALESCE(SUM(cost_usd), 0.0) FROM cost_events WHERE agent_id = ? AND ts >= ?",
            (agent_id, since),
        ).fetchone()
        return float(row[0])

    def check(self, agent_id: str) -> tuple[bool, Optional[str]]:
        """
        Returns (allowed, reason).
        allowed=True means the agent may proceed with the next LLM call.
        """
        rule = self._rules.get(agent_id)
        if rule is None:
            return True, None  # no budget rule configured — allow

        spent = self.rolling_cost(agent_id, rule.window_seconds)
        if spent >= rule.limit_usd:
            reason = (
                f"Agent '{agent_id}' has spent ${spent:.4f} in the last "
                f"{rule.window_seconds}s, exceeding the ${rule.limit_usd:.2f} limit."
            )
            return False, reason
        return True, None


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Usage in agent loop
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def run_agent_with_budget(agent_id: str, question: str) -> str:
    conn = init_db()
    controller = BudgetController(conn, rules=[
        BudgetRule(agent_id="research-agent-v2", window_seconds=3600, limit_usd=2.00),
        BudgetRule(agent_id="code-review-agent", window_seconds=86400, limit_usd=10.00),
    ])

    allowed, reason = controller.check(agent_id)
    if not allowed:
        return f"[BUDGET EXCEEDED] {reason} Task not started."

    # ... proceed with LLM call (would record cost event afterward) ...
    print(f"[OK] Agent '{agent_id}' approved. Proceeding with task.")
    # Simulate recording a cost event after the call:
    insert_cost_event(conn, {
        "ts": time.time(), "agent_id": agent_id, "task_id": "demo",
        "user_id": "u1", "team": "product", "model": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        "input_tok": 500, "output_tok": 200, "cost_usd": 0.0012,
    })
    return "Task completed."


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(run_agent_with_budget("research-agent-v2", "What is the boiling point of water?"))

Consider a B2B SaaS product that exposes five AI agents — document summarization, contract review, email drafting, code explanation, and data extraction — to customers on a usage-based pricing model. The platform processes 50 000 LLM calls per day. The engineering team needs to know the cost to serve each customer so they can bill correctly and identify customers whose usage is unprofitable.
At the request boundary — the FastAPI endpoint that handles each user request — the platform sets an attribution context containing the customer account ID mapped to
tied to the API request. All LLM calls made within that request's async context inherit these labels automatically via

# api_handler.py — FastAPI endpoint that sets attribution context per request
import uuid
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, Depends
from cost_tracker import set_attribution, AttributionContext, TrackedAnthropicClient

app = FastAPI()

def get_client() -> TrackedAnthropicClient:
    return TrackedAnthropicClient()

@app.post("/api/summarize")
async def summarize(
    request: Request,
    payload: dict,
    client: TrackedAnthropicClient = Depends(get_client),
) -> dict:
    # Extract customer identity from the authenticated session
    account_id = request.state.account_id  # set by auth middleware
    team = request.state.account_team

    # Set attribution for all LLM calls within this request context
    set_attribution(AttributionContext(
        agent_id="summarization-agent",
        task_id=f"task-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}",
        user_id=account_id,
        team=team,
        environment="production",
    ))

    # All LLM calls below are attributed to this customer + task
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Summarize the following document in three bullet points:\n\n{payload['text']}",
        }],
    )

    return {"summary": response.content[0].text}


# Nightly chargeback query (runs as a scheduled job)
CHARGEBACK_SQL = """
    SELECT
        user_id                           AS account_id,
        date(ts, 'unixepoch')            AS date,
        agent_id,
        SUM(input_tok)                   AS total_input_tokens,
        SUM(output_tok)                  AS total_output_tokens,
        ROUND(SUM(cost_usd), 4)          AS total_cost_usd
    FROM cost_events
    WHERE date(ts, 'unixepoch') = date('now', '-1 day')
    GROUP BY user_id, date, agent_id
    ORDER BY total_cost_usd DESC
"""

This query, run nightly against the cost event store, produces one row per (customer, agent, day). The billing system reads it to generate usage-based invoices. The operations team reads the same output to find the highest-spending customers and check whether their spending exceeds their contracted usage tier, triggering quota enforcement or a sales conversation.

Over 90 days of production data, the team discovers that the document summarization agent is consuming 40% of total platform LLM spend, predominantly from a single enterprise account that uploads very long contracts. This finding, invisible from the provider invoice alone, leads to implementing document chunking and caching for that account, reducing its per-document cost by 60%.

A common mistake is to record cost attribution once at the start of a run and assume it applies to all subsequent calls — but if the agent spawns sub-agents or uses libraries that create their own LLM clients, those calls bypass the attribution context. The fix is to ensure every LLM client used anywhere in the system is either the tracked wrapper or configured to forward attribution labels. The
approach handles this correctly for async code within the same Python process; calls to external microservices require forwarding attribution headers over HTTP.

Embedding price constants directly in agent code is fragile. When a provider updates its pricing or a new model is introduced, all references must be found and updated manually. The correct approach is to maintain a price schedule in configuration or in a database table, keyed by model name and effective date. Cost computation then looks up the current price at query time. This also enables historical cost recalculation when prices change.

Prompt caching on Anthropic's API reduces the effective price of re-used context significantly — cache read tokens cost roughly 10% of the input token price. An attribution system that counts all input tokens at the full input rate overstates costs for cache-heavy workloads. Always read
from the usage object and apply the correct rate.
Not enforcing attribution as a required field

If attribution context is optional, engineers working under deadline pressure will skip setting it. Over time, a growing fraction of cost events becomes unattributed, degrading the accuracy of all reports. Treat attribution context as required: raise an exception or emit a loud warning when an LLM call is made with no attribution context set. A sentinel value like
is better than silence, but making it hard to omit is better still.
Conflating cost attribution with observability tracing

OpenTelemetry traces carry span IDs useful for debugging latency and errors, but a trace span is not a cost record. Trace data is typically sampled, short-lived, and not suited for financial aggregation. Cost attribution data should be written to a durable, non-sampled store with retention appropriate for financial records (commonly 7 years). The two systems can share context IDs — include the trace ID in the cost event — but they serve different purposes and should have separate storage.
Set attribution context at the outermost boundary.
The API handler, the Celery task, or the scheduled job that initiates work is the right place. Everything below it inherits the context automatically.
Include a run or trace ID in every cost event.
lets you reconstruct the exact sequence of LLM calls for a specific task execution, which is essential for debugging cost anomalies and for linking cost data to quality metrics.

Store effective dates alongside each model's prices. When prices change, add a new row rather than updating the old one. Historical cost records remain accurate when re-computed against the prices that applied at the time.

Write raw cost events to an append-only event stream. Aggregations (daily rollups, per-team totals) are computed from the raw events. This preserves the ability to re-aggregate along any new dimension added later, and to correct errors by replaying events.
Alert on anomalies, not just thresholds.

A fixed threshold alert fires only when spend exceeds a static limit. An anomaly alert compares current spend to a rolling baseline (e.g., seven-day average for the same agent) and fires when spend deviates by more than 2x. This catches runaway agents even before they hit a hard limit.
Use soft limits before hard limits.
(e.g., at 80% of budget) triggers a switch to a cheaper model. A
(at 100%) blocks new calls. This graduated response prevents task failures while still enforcing the budget.
Run a daily query that computes the fraction of total cost_usd attributed to
. If this fraction exceeds 5%, investigate and fix the code paths that are bypassing attribution. Track this metric over time to catch regressions.
Cost attribution adds negligible latency to the critical path. The instrumentation work — reading

, computing a cost (a few multiplications), reading the ContextVar, and constructing a cost event dataclass — takes under 100 microseconds. The expensive part is writing the event to the database, and that write should be async and fire-and-forget so it does not extend the latency of the LLM response return to the caller.

At scale, cost event throughput grows proportionally with LLM call volume. A platform making 50 000 LLM calls per day produces 50 000 cost events per day — roughly 0.6 per second on average. This is a low write rate for any database. Even a modestly-provisioned PostgreSQL instance handles this comfortably with no special tuning.

For very high-volume platforms (millions of calls per day), buffer cost events in memory and flush in batches of 100–1 000 to the database. This trades a small delay in cost visibility for dramatically lower write overhead.
Use a columnar store for analytics.

Daily chargeback and cost-by-agent queries scan large volumes of cost event rows. A columnar data warehouse (ClickHouse, BigQuery, Redshift, DuckDB) executes these GROUP BY queries orders of magnitude faster than a row-oriented OLTP database. Stream raw events to the warehouse nightly or in micro-batches.

The budget controller must check the rolling spend for an agent on every LLM call. If this requires a SUM query over the raw events table, it adds a database round-trip to every call. Maintain a per-agent rolling total in Redis with TTL matching the budget window. Update it with each cost event. The budget check then becomes a single Redis GET — sub-millisecond.
For a PostgreSQL cost event store, partition the table by
. Budget window queries that filter on
scan only today's partition rather than the full history, keeping query times stable as data accumulates.
Captures token usage and cost per LangChain run with project-level aggregation; built-in dashboards for cost trends.
Traces, token counts, and costs per trace. Self-hostable. Supports custom model pricing. Native SDK integrations for Anthropic, OpenAI, and others.
Propagates trace context (and custom attribution attributes) across services. GenAI semantic conventions define standard span attributes for LLM calls.
HTTP proxy that sits between your code and the LLM API. Records all requests/responses, computes costs, and provides dashboards — with zero code changes to the application.
Analytical storage for cost events at scale. GROUP BY queries across millions of events complete in under a second. Native support for time-partitioned tables.
Expose cost totals as Prometheus gauges (per agent, per team). Grafana dashboards show real-time cost trends and alert on anomalies via Alertmanager.
Fast rolling-window budget totals. INCRBYFLOAT on a keyed counter with TTL matching the budget window enables sub-millisecond budget checks without a DB query.
Thread-local and async-safe storage for attribution context. A ContextVar set in an async task is automatically inherited by all sub-coroutines.
Developer visibility and debugging; not a billing system
Small organizations with hard team boundaries

In-process attribution is the most precise and flexible approach, at the cost of instrumentation effort. LLM proxies offer an excellent quick-start: configure Helicone or LiteLLM in front of the provider, pass a

HTTP header on each call, and per-agent cost dashboards appear with zero application code changes. For teams that need programmatic enforcement (blocking a specific agent before it exceeds budget), the in-process approach is necessary, as proxies typically support only coarser rate limiting.

Cost event data is financial data. Treat it accordingly. The attribution labels attached to cost events — user IDs, account IDs, team names — are personally identifying or commercially sensitive depending on the deployment. Apply the same access controls to the cost event store that would apply to billing records: restrict read access to finance, ops, and authorized engineers. Avoid including the actual content of prompts or responses in cost events; those belong in a separately-controlled observability store with its own retention and access policy.
Use append-only storage for cost events.

Financial audit requirements typically mandate that billing records cannot be deleted or modified. Configure the cost event table as append-only. Corrections are new records, not updates to existing ones.

If the cost event write fails (database unavailable, queue full), the LLM call has already completed and been charged by the provider. Log the cost event to a local fallback file and replay it when the store is available. Missing cost events create unattributed spend and corrupt budget enforcement.

If a bug causes the budget controller check to be skipped — for example, an exception swallowed in middleware — the agent proceeds without enforcement. Structure the budget check so that a failure to check means the call is blocked by default, not allowed. Fail-safe over fail-open.

If the cost computation layer encounters a model not in the price schedule, it should log a warning with the model name and record the cost as zero rather than crashing. A new model introduced mid-day should not take down the attribution system; update the price schedule and re-compute affected events.

Monthly, compare the total spend recorded in your cost event store against the provider's actual invoice. Discrepancies above 1–2% indicate missing events, incorrect pricing, or rounding errors. Build a reconciliation report and investigate gaps before they accumulate.

Cost attribution tags every LLM API call with organizational dimensions — agent, task, user, team — making it possible to answer "who spent what" at any granularity rather than seeing only a total account invoice.
is the idiomatic mechanism for async code: set the attribution context once at the request boundary and all coroutines that run within it inherit the labels without explicit parameter passing.

Token costs are linear — compute them from the model's price schedule applied to the input, cached, and output token counts returned in every API response. Maintain the price schedule in configuration with effective dates so historical costs remain accurate when providers change their pricing.

Write raw cost events to an append-only store and compute aggregations from them. This preserves the ability to re-aggregate by new dimensions, correct errors by replaying events, and satisfy financial audit requirements.

Budget enforcement requires per-dimension rolling totals: use Redis INCRBYFLOAT with a TTL equal to the budget window for sub-millisecond budget checks on each LLM call, without a database round-trip.

LLM proxy tools (Helicone, LiteLLM) provide per-tag cost dashboards with zero application code changes, and are an excellent starting point before building in-process attribution; in-process attribution becomes necessary when programmatic per-agent enforcement is required.

Reconcile the cost event store against provider invoices monthly; discrepancies above a few percent indicate missing events, incorrect model pricing, or ignored cached-token discounts that compound into significant inaccuracies over time.
